title: GuardPharma: Two-Tier Compositional Verification of Polypharmacy Safety via Pharmacokinetic Abstract Interpretation and Contract-Based Model Checking

overview.one_sentence: A two-tier formal verification engine that screens multi-guideline polypharmacy conflicts in seconds via pharmacokinetic abstract interpretation (Tier 1), then produces provably reachable counterexample trajectories for flagged conflicts via contract-based compositional model checking over Pharmacological Timed Automata (Tier 2).
overview.problem: Clinical guidelines are authored in isolation. No tooling verifies that N simultaneously active guidelines produce safe drug regimens for multimorbid patients across all possible trajectories. Polypharmacy ADEs are a leading cause of hospitalization in older adults.
overview.gap: No existing system combines (i) temporal reasoning over pharmacokinetic constraints, (ii) N-guideline composition into a verifiable model, (iii) sound verification with counterexample generation, and (iv) contract-based decomposition for scalability.
overview.contribution: The PTA formalism, CYP-enzyme interface contracts with Metzler monotonicity, two-tier architecture, and end-to-end verification pipeline.

core_formalism.name: Pharmacological Timed Automaton (PTA)

definition.tuple: (L, l_0, X, C, Phi, E, I, M_pk, S_clin)

components.L: Finite set of locations (clinical states: e.g., on_metformin, on_amlodipine, dose_adjusted)
components.X: Finite set of clock variables (time since drug initiation, time since last dose, time since lab check)
components.C: Finite set of continuous PK state variables c_1, ..., c_D representing drug plasma concentrations
components.Phi: Bounded population PK parameter space [phi_lo, phi_hi] in R^p
components.E: Set of edges (l, g, r, l') where g is a guard over X, C, S_clin and r is a reset/action
components.I: Location invariants I(l) constraining time spent in each location
components.M_pk: Pharmacokinetic dynamics model: dc/dt = M(u,phi)*c + B*d(t) where M is Metzler
components.S_clin: Discrete clinical state (conditions, lab value ranges, medication status flags)

semantics.state: (l, v, c, s) where l in L, v: X -> R_>=0 (clock valuation), c in R_>=0^D (concentrations), s in S_clin
semantics.time_evolution: In location l, clocks advance uniformly, concentrations evolve per dc/dt = M(u,phi)*c + B*d(t), clinical state s is constant
semantics.discrete_transitions: Edge (l,g,r,l') fires when guard g(v,c,s) is satisfied; resets clocks per r, may update d(t) (new drug dose), update s
semantics.runs: Alternating sequences of time-elapse and discrete transitions, starting from (l_0, 0, c_0, s_0)

core_formalism.key_property: The Metzler structure of M guarantees componentwise monotonicity of concentration solutions: if c(0) <= c'(0) componentwise, then c(t) <= c'(t) for all t >= 0. This is the foundation for contract-based composition.

parameters.CL_i: Clearance of drug i, modulated by CYP enzyme activity

one_compartment.metzler_form: For D drugs: dc/dt = M(u)*c + b(t), where M(u) is D x D Metzler matrix (diagonal entries negative = elimination, off-diagonal entries >= 0 = metabolite conversion)

competitive.meaning: Drug j's concentration I_j inhibits the CYP enzyme metabolizing drug i, reducing clearance
competitive.K_ij: Inhibition constant (drug j inhibiting drug i's metabolism)
competitive.monotonicity: Higher I_j -> lower CL_i -> higher C_ss_i -> higher I_i (if drug i also inhibits). This is the monotone chain enabling contract composition.

noncompetitive.note: Monotonicity may NOT hold for non-competitive inhibition. These interactions are routed to monolithic verification.

cyp_inhibition.coverage: Competitive CYP inhibition covers approximately 70% of clinically significant PK drug-drug interactions. The remaining 30% (enzyme induction, PD interactions like QT prolongation, serotonin syndrome) require direct product-PTA verification.

metzler_theory.definition: A matrix M in R^{n x n} is Metzler if all off-diagonal entries are non-negative: M_ij >= 0 for i != j
key_properties: The solution c(t) = exp(Mt)*c(0) + integral_0^t exp(M(t-s))*b(s) ds preserves non-negativity: c(0) >= 0 implies c(t) >= 0 for all t >= 0
Componentwise monotonicity: c(0) <= c'(0) implies exp(Mt)*c(0) <= exp(Mt)*c'(0)
If all eigenvalues of M have negative real part (asymptotically stable), then c(t) -> -M^{-1}*b as t -> infinity (steady state)
The steady-state is monotone in M: if M_1 <= M_2 (componentwise), then -M_1^{-1}*b >= -M_2^{-1}*b (lower clearance -> higher steady-state concentration)
metzler_theory.relevance: PK dynamics matrices are Metzler because: (a) diagonal entries are negative (drug elimination), (b) off-diagonal entries are non-negative (metabolite formation). All drugs are eventually eliminated, so M is asymptotically stable. These properties enable sound contract extraction and the convergence guarantee for abstract interpretation.

statement.informal: N-guideline verification decomposes into N individual guideline checks plus M enzyme-compatibility checks, reducing exponential product-automaton cost to polynomial cost, provided all CYP-enzyme interface contracts are mutually compatible under competitive inhibition.
statement.formal: Let P_1, ..., P_N be PTAs for N concurrent guidelines. For each CYP enzyme e in E_shared (shared by subset S_e of guidelines), define enzyme-interface contract C_{e,i} = (A_{e,i}, G_{e,i}) where A_{e,i} specifies minimum assumed enzyme-e activity from environment and G_{e,i} specifies maximum enzyme-e load from P_i's medications. Under Assumptions A1-A3 (competitive CYP inhibition, Metzler dynamics, bounded PK parameters), the composition P_1 || ... || P_N satisfies safety property phi if: (1) For each i, P_i |= phi_i under contract C_{e,i} for all e, and (2) For each enzyme e, sum_{j in S_e, j!=i} G_{e,j}(min_capacity) <= A_{e,i} for all i in S_e (mutual compatibility).

lemma_1_1.name: Concentration Monotonicity in Enzyme Capacity
lemma_1_1.statement: For drug i with CYP-mediated clearance, C_ss_i is monotonically decreasing in enzyme activity E_e: lower enzyme capacity -> higher steady-state concentration.
lemma_1_1.proof: C_ss_i = (F_i * dose_i) / (CL_i(E_e) * tau_i). Under competitive inhibition, CL_i(E_e) = CL_i^0 * E_e / E_max where E_e is the available enzyme activity. Since F_i, dose_i, tau_i > 0, C_ss_i is proportional to 1/CL_i(E_e), which is proportional to 1/E_e. Thus C_ss_i is monotonically decreasing in E_e. For transient dynamics, the Metzler property of M(E_e) ensures componentwise monotonicity: if E_e^1 <= E_e^2, then M(E_e^1) <= M(E_e^2) componentwise (lower enzyme activity means less negative diagonal = slower elimination), and by the comparison theorem for Metzler ODEs, c(t; E_e^1) >= c(t; E_e^2) for all t >= 0.

lemma_1_2.name: Enzyme Load Monotonicity in Concentration
lemma_1_2.statement: For competitive CYP inhibition, the enzyme load (inhibition fraction) imposed by drug j is monotonically increasing in c_j.
lemma_1_2.proof: Inhibition fraction f_j(c_j) = c_j / (c_j + K_j) (Michaelis-Menten). The derivative df_j/dc_j = K_j / (c_j + K_j)^2 > 0 for all c_j >= 0 and K_j > 0. Thus f_j is strictly monotonically increasing in c_j. The total enzyme load from guideline i is L_i = sum_j f_j(c_j) where the sum is over drugs j prescribed by guideline i. Since each f_j is increasing in c_j, and c_j is increasing in enzyme load from other guidelines (by Lemma 1.1), L_i is monotonically increasing in the external enzyme load.

lemma_1_3.name: Soundness of Worst-Case Guarantee Computation
lemma_1_3.statement: Computing G_{e,i} under minimum assumed enzyme capacity (maximum external inhibition) yields a sound upper bound on guideline i's actual enzyme load in any compatible composition.
lemma_1_3.proof: By Lemma 1.1, minimum enzyme capacity produces maximum drug concentrations. By Lemma 1.2, maximum drug concentrations produce maximum enzyme load. Therefore G_{e,i}(min_capacity) >= G_{e,i}(actual_capacity) for any actual enzyme capacity >= min_capacity. This resolves the circular dependency (concentrations depend on enzyme activity, which depends on concentrations) in a single pass: compute worst-case concentrations assuming minimum enzyme capacity, then derive worst-case enzyme loads. The Metzler monotonicity ensures this single-pass computation is sound without fixed-point iteration.

lemma_1_4.name: Contract Compatibility Implies Product Safety
lemma_1_4.statement: If all enzyme-interface contracts are mutually compatible (condition 2) and each guideline satisfies its local safety property under its contract (condition 1), then the product P_1 || ... || P_N satisfies the global safety property phi.
lemma_1_4.proof: By contract compatibility, the actual enzyme load from guidelines j != i is at most sum_{j!=i} G_{e,j}(min_capacity) <= A_{e,i}. By soundness of worst-case guarantees (Lemma 1.3), the actual enzyme load is bounded by the worst-case guarantee. Therefore the actual enzyme activity available to guideline i is at least A_{e,i}, which is the assumed capacity under which P_i was verified safe. By the assume-guarantee principle (Pnueli 1985, Giannakopoulou et al. 2005), if P_i satisfies phi_i assuming enzyme capacity >= A_{e,i}, and the environment provides enzyme capacity >= A_{e,i}, then P_i satisfies phi_i in the composition. Since phi = conjunction of phi_i for all i, the product satisfies phi.

proof_structure.main_proof: Theorem 1 follows from Lemmas 1.1-1.4. Conditions (1) and (2) are sufficient for safety of the N-guideline composition. Verification cost: N individual guideline checks (each polynomial in single-guideline state space) + N*M enzyme-compatibility checks (each O(N) arithmetic operations per enzyme). Total: O(N * V_single + N^2 * M) where V_single is the cost of verifying one guideline. Compare with monolithic product: O(V_single^N) — exponential.
proof_structure.where_proof_breaks: For non-competitive inhibition: CL_i(E_e) = CL_i^0 * K_ij / (c_j + K_ij). The monotonicity chain in Lemma 1.2 may not hold because the inhibition kinetics differ. For enzyme induction: higher inducer concentration -> higher enzyme activity -> lower co-drug concentration. This reverses the monotonicity direction, invalidating Lemma 1.1. For pharmacodynamic interactions (QT prolongation, serotonin syndrome): these do not involve CYP enzymes at all, so the enzyme-interface abstraction is inapplicable. All such interactions are routed to monolithic product-PTA verification.

complexity.reduction: Exponential to polynomial in N for the ~70% of interactions covered by competitive CYP inhibition

statement.informal: The PK-aware widening operator on the abstract domain converges in at most O(D*k) iterations, where D is the number of drugs and k is the maximum enzyme-coupling degree.
statement.formal: Define widening operator nabla_PK on abstract domain A = [c_lo, c_hi]^D x [E_lo, E_hi]^M x 2^P_clin as: for concentration intervals, widen to [0, C_ss_max(phi_worst)]; for enzyme-activity intervals, widen to [E_min(full_inhibition), E_max(no_inhibition)]. The abstract fixed-point computation F^nabla(a_0) converges in at most D + D*k iterations, where k = max_{e in E} |S_e| is the maximum enzyme-coupling degree.

proof_structure.soundness: The widened intervals [0, C_ss_max] and [E_min, E_max] are sound over-approximations because: (a) drug concentrations are non-negative (Metzler property preserves non-negativity), (b) C_ss_max(phi_worst) is the worst-case steady-state under worst-case population PK parameters, which upper-bounds all transient and steady-state concentrations for the 1-compartment model, (c) E_min and E_max bound enzyme activity under full inhibition and no inhibition respectively.

convergence.non_interacting_drugs: For drugs not sharing CYP enzymes, each drug's concentration interval independently reaches [0, C_ss_max] in 1 widening iteration (closed-form steady state from Metzler eigenvalue analysis). This contributes D iterations.
convergence.enzyme_coupled_drugs: For drugs sharing enzyme e, convergence is a coupled fixed-point. Each widening iteration updates enzyme-load intervals based on previous concentration intervals, then updates concentration intervals based on new enzyme-activity intervals. Metzler monotonicity ensures the sequence of enzyme-load intervals is monotonically increasing and bounded by total enzyme capacity. At most k iterations per coupling layer suffice because: (i) each iteration strictly increases at least one enzyme-load lower bound, (ii) there are at most k drugs per enzyme, (iii) the sequence is bounded. Total additional iterations: D*k.
convergence.total: D + D*k = O(D*k) iterations. Each iteration costs O(|L| * D) abstract transfer function evaluations.

proof_structure.fallback: If the O(D*k) bound is exceeded (indicating numerical instability or an error in the coupling analysis), the widening operator falls back to conservative bounds: concentration intervals widen to [0, LD50_i] (lethal dose), enzyme intervals widen to [0, E_max]. This is sound but imprecise, producing more 'possibly unsafe' verdicts that Tier 2 must resolve.

observation_3.name: delta-Decidability of PTA Reachability

statement.informal: PTA reachability is delta-decidable: for any delta > 0, the verifier answers 'unsafe' (exact) or 'delta-safe' (safe up to perturbation delta).
statement.formal: Let P be a PTA with Metzler ODE dynamics dc/dt = M(u,phi)*c + B*d(t) where phi in Phi = [phi_lo, phi_hi]. The reachability problem 'does there exist phi in Phi and a dosing schedule consistent with the guideline such that an unsafe location l_unsafe is reached?' is delta-decidable for any delta > 0.

observation_3.proof: Direct application of Gao et al.'s dReal delta-decidability framework (LICS 2012, IJCAR 2012). PK dynamics with Metzler ODEs fall within dReal's decidable fragment (bounded ODEs with Lipschitz-continuous right-hand side over compact domains). The PTA's hybrid structure (discrete locations + continuous ODE evolution) maps to dReal's hybrid automaton encoding. No new proof technique is involved.

clinical_calibration.rationale: Ensures 'delta-safe' means safe up to a perturbation below the threshold of pharmacological significance. For most drugs, this yields delta ~ 0.1-1.0 ug/mL.
clinical_calibration.narrow_therapeutic_index: For NTI drugs (digoxin: range 0.8-2.0 ng/mL, lithium: range 0.6-1.2 mEq/L), delta_i may be sub-nanogram, which strains validated interval arithmetic precision. Mitigation: for NTI drugs, use direct SAT-based BMC with exact rational arithmetic rather than interval ODE integration.
clinical_calibration.overall_delta: delta = min_i(delta_i) across all drugs in the verification instance

algorithm_1_tier1_screening.name: PK Abstract Interpretation Screening
algorithm_1_tier1_screening.input: Set of N guideline PTAs, PK model library, patient comorbidity profile
algorithm_1_tier1_screening.output: For each guideline pair: definitely-safe / possibly-unsafe / definitely-unsafe

abstract_domain.carrier: Product lattice: [c_lo, c_hi]^D x [E_lo, E_hi]^M x 2^P_clin
abstract_domain.join: Interval union for concentrations and enzyme activities; set union for clinical predicates
abstract_domain.widening: PK-aware: widen concentrations to [0, C_ss_max(phi_worst)], enzymes to [E_min, E_max]
abstract_domain.narrowing: Post-widening narrowing using Metzler steady-state lower bounds to tighten concentration intervals

transfer_functions.drug_initiation: Set c_i to [0, F_i*dose_i/V_i] (peak after first dose); update medication flags
transfer_functions.time_elapse: Propagate concentration intervals via interval matrix exponential: c(t) in [exp(M_lo*t)*c_lo, exp(M_hi*t)*c_hi + integral bounds]
transfer_functions.dose_adjustment: Update dosing input b(t); recompute steady-state bounds
transfer_functions.lab_check: Narrow clinical predicate set based on lab-value guard evaluation
transfer_functions.drug_discontinuation: Set c_i to current interval; apply elimination dynamics (exponential decay)

classification.definitely_safe: Abstract reachable set is entirely within safe region (all concentration intervals below therapeutic ceiling, no unsafe clinical predicate reachable)
classification.possibly_unsafe: Abstract reachable set intersects unsafe region but is not entirely within it
classification.definitely_unsafe: Abstract lower bound of some concentration exceeds toxic threshold (requires enzyme-coupled dynamics to achieve)

algorithm_1_tier1_screening.complexity: O(|L|^2 * D * k) per fixed-point iteration, O(D*k) iterations. Total: O(|L|^2 * D^2 * k^2). For typical guidelines: |L| ~ 10-30, D ~ 5-15, k ~ 3-5. Expected wall-clock: <5 seconds for 20 guidelines on laptop CPU.

algorithm_2_contract_extraction.name: CYP-Enzyme Interface Contract Extraction
algorithm_2_contract_extraction.input: Single guideline PTA P_i, PK model library, set of shared CYP enzymes
algorithm_2_contract_extraction.output: For each shared enzyme e: contract C_{e,i} = (A_{e,i}, G_{e,i})
steps: 1. Identify drugs prescribed by guideline P_i that are substrates or inhibitors of shared CYP enzymes
2. For each enzyme e: set assumed enzyme capacity to minimum (maximum external inhibition from other guidelines)
3. Compute worst-case drug concentrations under minimum enzyme capacity using Metzler steady-state: C_ss_worst = -(M(E_min))^{-1} * b_max
4. Compute guarantee G_{e,i} = sum_j f_j(C_ss_worst_j) where f_j is the Michaelis-Menten inhibition fraction for drug j on enzyme e
5. Compute assumption A_{e,i} = E_max - G_{e,i} (enzyme capacity needed from environment = total capacity minus own load)
6. Return contracts for all shared enzymes
algorithm_2_contract_extraction.single_pass_justification: Metzler monotonicity (Lemma 1.3) ensures worst-case concentrations computed under minimum enzyme capacity are sound upper bounds. No iteration needed.
algorithm_2_contract_extraction.complexity: O(D_i * M_i) per guideline, where D_i = drugs in guideline i, M_i = shared enzymes for guideline i

algorithm_3_compatibility_check.name: Contract Compatibility Verification
algorithm_3_compatibility_check.input: N sets of enzyme-interface contracts {C_{e,i}}, one per guideline per shared enzyme
algorithm_3_compatibility_check.output: Compatible (with safety certificate) or Incompatible (with minimal conflicting subset)
steps: 1. For each shared enzyme e:
a. Collect all guarantees G_{e,i} for i in S_e
b. For each i in S_e: check sum_{j in S_e, j!=i} G_{e,j} <= A_{e,i}
c. If any check fails: record (e, i, failing_guarantees) as incompatibility
2. If all checks pass: emit safety certificate (conjunction of all contracts)
3. If any check fails: identify minimal conflicting subset via greedy removal
4. Route incompatible subsets to Tier 2 monolithic verification
algorithm_3_compatibility_check.complexity: O(N^2 * M) where N = guidelines, M = shared enzymes. Dominated by contract extraction cost.

certificate_format.safe: JSON object: {enzyme: e, guidelines: [i1,...,ik], total_load: L, capacity: E_max, margin: E_max - L}
certificate_format.conflict: JSON object: {enzyme: e, guidelines: [i1,...,ik], total_load: L, capacity: E_max, overflow: L - E_max, drugs: [d1,...,dm]}

algorithm_4_bmc.name: SAT-Based Bounded Model Checking
algorithm_4_bmc.input: Product PTA (or enzyme-group PTA), safety property phi, time horizon T
algorithm_4_bmc.output: Safe (within horizon T) or Counterexample trace

encoding.time_discretization: Hourly steps for first 7 days (acute interactions), 6-hourly for days 7-30, daily for days 30-365
encoding.state_variables: Per drug: concentration (rational encoding at clinical threshold granularity). Per enzyme: activity level. Per location: Boolean. Per clock: integer (time steps since reset)
encoding.transition_relation: T(s_t, s_{t+1}): encodes PTA edge firing + ODE step (matrix exponential for 1-compartment, precomputed as rational approximation)
encoding.safety_property: phi_unsafe encoded as conjunction of threshold predicates: c_i > toxic_threshold_i for some i
encoding.bmc_unrolling: exists s_0, s_1, ..., s_T: I(s_0) AND T(s_0,s_1) AND ... AND T(s_{T-1},s_T) AND phi_unsafe(s_T)

algorithm_4_bmc.backend: Z3 (SMT-LIB2 encoding with QF_LRA logic for rational PK arithmetic) or CaDiCaL (propositional encoding with bit-blasting for fixed-point concentration arithmetic)
algorithm_4_bmc.complexity: O(T * |S|^2 * D) per BMC query. T = number of time steps (variable: ~200-8760), |S| = state space per step, D = drugs. Worst case: hours for 5+ interacting drugs. Expected case: seconds to minutes for 2-3 drug interactions.
when_to_use: Contract compatibility check fails (confirms whether conflict is real or due to over-approximation)
Tier 1 flags 'possibly unsafe' and contract path is unavailable

algorithm_5_cegar.name: Clinical-Domain CEGAR Refinement
algorithm_5_cegar.input: Abstract PTA model, safety property, initial abstraction
abstraction_hierarchy: Level 0: Drug-class abstraction (all statins -> one abstract statin, all ACE inhibitors -> one abstract ACEI)
Level 1: Individual drug identity (atorvastatin vs. simvastatin)
Level 2: Dose-specific thresholds (low-dose vs. high-dose atorvastatin)
Level 3: Lab-value refinement (eGFR < 30 vs. eGFR 30-60 vs. eGFR >= 60)
refinement_loop: 1. Model check abstract PTA against safety property
2. If safe: return safe verdict (sound due to over-approximation)
3. If unsafe: extract abstract counterexample trace
4. Check PK feasibility of abstract trace (simulate ODE along trace)
6. If spurious: identify abstraction point causing spuriousness
7. Refine abstraction (split abstract state at identified point)
algorithm_5_cegar.convergence: Worst case: exponential in abstraction depth (5 levels). Expected case: 2-3 refinement iterations for typical guideline pairs, because clinical abstractions (drug class, dose range) are coarse enough to resolve most spurious counterexamples at Level 1-2.
algorithm_5_cegar.complexity: Each iteration: O(V_abstract) where V_abstract is model checking cost at current abstraction level. Total iterations bounded by product of abstraction dimensions.

algorithm_6_counterexample.name: Counterexample Extraction and Clinical Narration
algorithm_6_counterexample.output: Minimal PTA trace + PK trajectory + clinical narrative
steps: 1. Extract full trace from SAT assignment: sequence of (location, clock_values, concentrations, clinical_state) at each time step
2. Minimize trace: remove time steps where no discrete transition fires and concentration changes are monotone (binary search on removal candidates)
3. Reconstruct PK trajectories: integrate ODEs along minimized trace for each drug, producing concentration-vs-time curves
4. Identify critical events: (a) threshold crossings (concentration exceeds toxic level), (b) enzyme saturation points, (c) drug initiation/discontinuation events
5. Generate clinical narrative: template-based NLG mapping formal events to clinical language
6. Output: JSON trace + SVG/PNG PK trajectory plots + human-readable narrative
algorithm_6_counterexample.narrative_template: Patient with [comorbidities] starts [drug_1] on day [t1]. On day [t2], [guideline_2] initiates [drug_2] (CYP[enzyme] inhibitor). By day [t3], [drug_1] plasma concentration reaches [c] [units], exceeding toxic threshold [threshold] [units]. Mechanism: [drug_2] inhibits CYP[enzyme]-mediated clearance of [drug_1], reducing clearance by [X]%.
algorithm_6_counterexample.complexity: O(T * D) for trace extraction, O(T * D^2) for ODE reconstruction. Dominated by BMC solving time.

algorithm_7_significance.name: Clinical Significance Ranking
algorithm_7_significance.input: Set of confirmed conflicts with counterexample traces
algorithm_7_significance.output: Ranked conflict list with severity tiers

comorbidity_prevalence.source: Medicare claims data (CMS public use files)

tiers.critical: composite >= 7.0 OR drugbank = major AND beers = listed

algorithm_8_pipeline.name: End-to-End Verification Pipeline
algorithm_8_pipeline.input: N guideline PTAs, patient comorbidity profile, PK model library
algorithm_8_pipeline.output: Safety report with certificates, conflicts, counterexamples, severity rankings
steps: Step 1: Tier 1 — Run abstract screening on all N*(N-1)/2 guideline pairs. Classify each as definitely-safe, possibly-unsafe, or definitely-unsafe.
Step 2: For definitely-safe pairs — emit safety certificate immediately.
Step 3: For all non-safe pairs — extract CYP-enzyme interface contracts.
Step 4: Group non-safe pairs by shared enzyme pathways. Run contract compatibility check per enzyme group.
Step 5: For compatible groups — emit compositional safety certificate.
Step 6: For incompatible groups — run Tier 2 BMC on the product PTA for the incompatible subset.
Step 7: For contract-ineligible pairs (PD interactions, enzyme induction) — run Tier 2 BMC directly.
Step 8: For confirmed conflicts — extract counterexamples, reconstruct PK trajectories, generate clinical narratives.
Step 9: Rank all conflicts by clinical significance.
Step 10: Generate final safety report.

decision_flow.tier1_safe_rate: Expected ~70-80% of pairs classified definitely-safe by Tier 1
decision_flow.contract_eligible_rate: ~70% of remaining pairs eligible for contract-based decomposition
decision_flow.contract_compatible_rate: Expected ~80-90% of contract-eligible pairs are compatible
decision_flow.bmc_required_rate: Expected ~5-10% of total pairs require full BMC

complexity_summary.comparison_with_monolithic: Monolithic product-PTA verification: O(|L|^N * D^N * T) — exponential in N. With contract decomposition: O(N * |L|^1 * D * T + N^2 * M) — polynomial in N. Speedup: exponential for the ~70% of interactions covered by contracts.

implementation_strategy.language: Rust (core verification engine) + Python (evaluation harness, plotting, clinical narrative templates)

observation_3: interval_ode_solver (deferred — use matrix exponential for MVP)

external_dependencies.Z3: SMT backend for BMC (MIT license, mature)
external_dependencies.CaDiCaL: SAT backend for propositional BMC encoding (MIT license)
external_dependencies.DrugBank: Drug interaction database (academic license, free for research)

experiments.name: Temporal Ablation (CENTERPIECE)
experiments.rq: Does temporal PK reasoning detect conflicts invisible to atemporal approaches?
experiments.design: Paired comparison: full Tier 2 (PK temporal dynamics) vs. atemporal checker (DrugBank interaction lookup, no timing/concentration)
experiments.metric: X% = fraction of conflicts found ONLY by temporal reasoning
experiments.statistical_method: McNemar's test for matched pairs, 95% CI for X%
experiments.fallback: If X < 15%, pivot to explanation quality (PK trajectory counterexamples provide mechanism detail regardless of detection rate)

experiments.name: Known-Conflict Recall
experiments.rq: Can GuardPharma detect known dangerous drug interactions?
experiments.design: Inject Beers 2023 DDIs and STOPP/START v3 contraindications into guideline pairs

experiments.name: Two-Tier Speedup
experiments.rq: Does Tier 1 screening meaningfully reduce verification time?
experiments.design: Measure Tier 1 + Tier 2 time vs. Tier-2-only time for guideline sets of size 2, 5, 10, 15, 20
experiments.metric: Speedup ratio (Tier-2-only / total), Tier 1 filter rate

experiments.name: Compositionality Speedup
experiments.rq: Does contract-based composition outperform monolithic verification?
experiments.design: Compositional (Theorem 1) vs. monolithic Tier 2 for same guideline sets

experiments.name: DrugBank Cross-Validation
experiments.design: Cross-reference all confirmed conflicts against DrugBank interaction database

experiments.name: FAERS Signal Validation
experiments.rq: Do discovered conflicts have real-world adverse event signals?
experiments.design: Check each conflict against pre-computed FAERS disproportionality table
experiments.metric: Fraction of novel conflicts with PRR > 2 and CI > 1

experiments.name: Tier 1 False-Positive Rate
experiments.rq: How precise is Tier 1 abstract screening?
experiments.design: Fraction of Tier 1 'possibly unsafe' verdicts resolved to safe by Tier 2

experiments.name: Clinical Significance Stratification
experiments.rq: Does the severity filter reduce alert fatigue?
experiments.design: Distribution of Critical / Significant / Informational across all conflicts

experiments.name: Clinical Pharmacist Review (MANDATORY)
experiments.rq: Do clinical experts agree detected conflicts are clinically relevant?
experiments.design: 3 pharmacists independently rate 20 conflicts on 5-point Likert scale

baselines.name: TMR-style atemporal checker
baselines.type: Atemporal rule-based checking using DrugBank interaction pairs

baselines.name: LLM+DrugBank
baselines.type: GPT-4 with DrugBank RAG for drug interaction checking

sources: Manually encoded clinical rules (~30: Wells, CHADS2-VASc, MELD, Beers-relevant drug protocols)
corpus.note: CMS eCQMs are retrospective quality measures, not treatment guidelines — included for compilation testing only

risk_registry.mitigation: Fallback narrative (explanation quality + compositionality speedup + existence proof). Pre-calibrate with synthetic temporal pairs.

risk_registry.risk: PTA encoding of real guidelines is infeasible
risk_registry.mitigation: Pilot gate: 3 guideline pairs before full commitment.
90-day bounded horizon always terminates. Report convergence statistics honestly.
Supplement with manually encoded rules. Frame as proof-of-concept.

risk_registry.risk: PopPK parameters unavailable for some drugs
risk_registry.mitigation: Curate ~50 most common drugs first. Conservative estimates for missing.
Conservative classification; uncertain interactions routed to monolithic.

risk_registry.risk: Validated interval ODE integration too slow
risk_registry.mitigation: Deferred for MVP; use 1-compartment matrix exponential.
